title: Apple Charlotte
subtitle: Buttered bread lining a basin filled with spiced apple, baked until golden and turned out
excerpt: A pudding basin lined with slices of buttered white bread, packed with cinnamon-spiced cooked Bramley apple, sealed with more bread, and baked until the outer casing is crisp and deeply golden. Turned out on a plate and served with cream or custard.
sourceNotes: Apple Charlotte appears in Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) and is attributed to the late eighteenth or early nineteenth century in British and French cookery. It is named for either Queen Charlotte, wife of George III, or Charlotte of Mecklenburg. The bread-lined basin with a fruit filling was a practical alternative to pastry that produced a self-contained pudding that could be turned out whole.

content.text: Apple Charlotte is built the wrong way up: the base of the basin becomes the top of the finished pudding when it is turned out. The bread slices need to overlap well in the basin so there are no gaps for the apple to escape through. Cut a circle for the base, then fit rectangular strips around the sides with each one overlapping its neighbour slightly.

The bread must be thoroughly buttered on one side. This is what gives the casing its colour and crispness during baking. Use white sandwich bread rather than sourdough or wholemeal, both are too dense and make the casing heavy rather than crisp.
A 1.2-litre pudding basin, buttered. You will also need a plate slightly smaller than the basin opening to weight the pudding down during baking.

Cook {{apple-bramley}} of prepared Bramley apple with {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar, the lemon zest and juice, and {{cinnamon-ground}} of ground cinnamon in a medium saucepan over medium heat, stirring occasionally, for 10, 15 minutes until the apple has collapsed to a thick, rough paste. The mixture should be dry enough to hold its shape on a spoon. If it is still very wet, cook for a few more minutes with the lid off. Remove from the heat and cool.

Heat the oven to 200°C fan. Butter one side of each bread slice with {{unsalted-butter}} of softened butter. Cut a circle from one slice to fit the base of the basin (butter side down). Cut the remaining slices into rectangular strips about 4 cm wide and line the sides of the basin, butter side against the basin, overlapping each strip by about 1 cm. Trim the tops level with the rim.

Pack the cooled apple filling firmly into the bread-lined basin. Cover the top with more buttered bread slices, butter side up, trimmed to fit and overlapping to seal. Place a small plate or disc of foil on top and press down firmly.

Bake for 35, 40 minutes until the bread is deep golden and the juices at the base are caramelised. Rest for 5 minutes in the basin. Run a palette knife around the inside edge, place a warmed serving plate over the basin, and invert confidently. The charlotte should drop out. Serve immediately with cold cream or custard.

items.fix: Overlap the bread strips by at least 1 cm with no visible gaps. The apple filling must be thick enough to hold its shape on a spoon before it goes into the basin.
items.cause: Bread gaps in the lining let the filling escape, or the filling was too wet

items.fix: Butter each slice generously, the bread should look visibly coated. Check the oven temperature; the casing needs 200°C fan to colour properly in 40 minutes.
items.cause: Butter on the bread was too thin, or the oven temperature was too low
items.symptom: Bread casing is pale and soft rather than golden and crisp

items.fix: The apple should be very thick, almost like a paste, before it goes into the basin. If the filling is still runny after 15 minutes, continue cooking with the lid off.
items.cause: Apple filling was not reduced enough before going into the basin
items.symptom: Filling is wet and loose inside the bread case

items.fix: Place the basin on a baking tray. If the base is colouring very fast in the first 20 minutes, lower the oven shelf.
items.cause: The base of the basin sits directly on the oven rack and browns faster
items.symptom: Base (which becomes the top) is much darker than the sides

content.text: Apple Charlotte is one of the older named British puddings. Recipes appear in cookery books from the early 1800s. The bread-lined basin was a practical fix in a time before home pastry was reliable. It turned out whole and held its shape on the plate. The dish appears in the Victorian household guides of Mrs Beeton and was made through the late 1800s and early 1900s. The crumble has taken its place in most modern kitchens, but a Charlotte still looks dramatic when it comes to the table.
